Building Modern Web Scrapers with TypeScript

By Jane Developer | November 15, 2025

Web scraping has evolved significantly over the past decade. What once required fragile regular expressions and manual HTTP requests can now be accomplished with sophisticated tools that understand page structure, handle JavaScript rendering, and extract meaningful content from the noise of modern web pages.

In this guide, we will walk through the fundamentals of building a robust web scraper using TypeScript. We will cover everything from making HTTP requests to parsing HTML, handling dynamic content, and storing extracted data efficiently.

Why TypeScript for Web Scraping?

TypeScript brings several advantages to web scraping projects. Static typing catches errors at compile time rather than runtime, which is especially valuable when dealing with unpredictable web content. The rich ecosystem of npm packages provides tools for every aspect of the scraping pipeline.

Additionally, TypeScript's excellent async/await support makes it natural to write concurrent scrapers that can fetch multiple pages simultaneously without blocking.

Setting Up Your Project

Start by initializing a new TypeScript project with the following dependencies:

  • node-fetch or the built-in fetch API for HTTP requests
  • cheerio for fast HTML parsing without a browser
  • playwright for pages that require JavaScript rendering
  • better-sqlite3 for local data storage

The combination of these tools gives you a flexible foundation that can handle both static and dynamic websites.

Handling Dynamic Content

Many modern websites rely heavily on JavaScript to render their content. A simple HTTP request will only return the initial HTML shell, missing the actual content that gets loaded by client-side scripts. This is where browser automation tools like Playwright become essential.

Playwright launches a real browser instance, executes JavaScript, and gives you access to the fully rendered DOM. While slower than direct HTTP requests, it ensures you capture all the content a user would see.

Learn more about Playwright

Extracting Meaningful Content

The real challenge in web scraping is not fetching pages but extracting the meaningful content from them. Modern web pages are filled with navigation menus, advertisements, sidebars, and footer content that you typically want to exclude.

Libraries like Readability and Defuddle solve this problem by analyzing the page structure and identifying the main content area. They use heuristics based on element density, text length, and structural patterns to separate signal from noise.

Conclusion

Building a web scraper with TypeScript provides a solid foundation for extracting structured data from the web. By combining HTTP clients with smart content extraction, you can build tools that reliably convert messy web pages into clean, usable data.

The key is to start simple and add complexity only as needed. Begin with direct HTTP requests and basic parsing, then add browser automation and sophisticated extraction only for the pages that require it.
